static.description: A polyglot microservices platform for food delivery, consisting of 5 independent services (API Gateway, Order, Inventory, Driver, Notification) across Kotlin, Java, Python, Go, and TypeScript stacks, orchestrated via Docker Compose with RabbitMQ as the async event bus.
domain_concepts: OrderStatus: Enum representing the lifecycle states of an order: PENDING → ACCEPTED → PREPARING → READY_FOR_PICKUP → IN_TRANSIT → DELIVERED, with CANCELLED and REFUNDED as terminal/side states.
Order: JPA entity representing a customer's food order, containing items, delivery address, total amount, Stripe PaymentIntent ID, and current lifecycle status.
OrderItem: Embeddable value object within an Order representing a single line item with menu item reference, name, quantity, and unit price.
Restaurant: SQLAlchemy model representing a food establishment with name, address, cuisine type, rating, and associated menu items.
MenuItem: SQLAlchemy model for a restaurant menu item with price and stock quantity (-1 means unlimited availability), requiring Redis distributed locks for finite stock reservation.
Driver: MongoDB document representing a delivery driver with name, phone, vehicle type, availability status, and GPS location.
GeoPoint: Embedded struct in the Driver document storing latitude/longitude GPS coordinates for driver location tracking.
JwtAuthFilter: Spring Cloud Gateway global filter that validates JWT tokens on incoming requests and forwards the verified customer ID as X-Customer-Id header to downstream services.
order.events: RabbitMQ exchange used as the async event bus for order lifecycle events, consumed by the Notification Service.
acquire_lock: Redis-based distributed lock mechanism in the Inventory Service to prevent concurrent overselling of finite stock items.
isValidTransition: Static method on OrderStatus that enforces the allowed state machine transitions in the order lifecycle.
stripePaymentIntentId: Stripe PaymentIntent ID (pi_xxxx) stored on an Order, used for manual capture and refund operations.

dynamic.architecture_summary: The system follows a microservices architecture with an API Gateway (Kotlin/Spring Cloud Gateway) that performs JWT authentication and routes requests to downstream services. The Order Service (Java/Spring Boot) manages order lifecycle with Stripe payments and publishes events to RabbitMQ, which the Notification Service (Node.js/TypeScript) consumes to send SMS/email notifications. The Inventory Service (Python/FastAPI) handles restaurant menus with Redis distributed locks for stock reservation, and the Driver Service (Go) manages driver profiles and location tracking via MongoDB.

key_components.name: GatewayApplication
key_components.responsibility: Entry point for the Kotlin Spring Cloud Gateway that routes and load-balances requests to downstream microservices.

key_components.name: JwtAuthFilter
key_components.responsibility: Global gateway filter that validates JWT tokens and forwards the authenticated customer ID to downstream services via X-Customer-Id header.

key_components.name: OrderServiceApplication
key_components.responsibility: Spring Boot entry point for the Order Service managing order CRUD and lifecycle.

key_components.name: OrderController
key_components.responsibility: REST controller exposing endpoints for creating, listing, retrieving, updating status, and cancelling orders.

key_components.name: OrderService
key_components.responsibility: Business logic for order creation with Stripe payment intents, status transitions with validation, and RabbitMQ event publishing.

key_components.name: Order
key_components.responsibility: JPA entity modeling a food delivery order with embedded line items, Stripe payment reference, and audit timestamps.

key_components.name: OrderStatus
key_components.responsibility: Enum defining the order lifecycle state machine and valid state transitions.

key_components.name: main.py (Inventory Service)
key_components.responsibility: FastAPI application handling restaurant/menu queries and stock reservation with Redis distributed locks.

key_components.name: models.py
key_components.responsibility: SQLAlchemy ORM models for Restaurant and MenuItem entities with PostgreSQL UUID primary keys.

key_components.name: main.go (Driver Service)
key_components.responsibility: Go HTTP service for driver profile CRUD and location tracking backed by MongoDB with graceful shutdown.

key_components.name: index.ts (Notification Service)
key_components.responsibility: TypeScript service consuming RabbitMQ order events and dispatching SMS (Twilio) and email (SendGrid) notifications with idempotency guards.

agent_safe_operations: Add new REST endpoints to existing service controllers
Add new unit or integration tests in any service
Modify business logic in OrderService, Inventory main.py, or Driver main.go
Add new model fields (non-breaking) to Order, MenuItem, Restaurant, or Driver
Update application configuration in application.properties or application.yml
Add new Python dependencies to inventory-service/requirements.txt
Add new npm dependencies to notification-service/package.json
Create new Go source files in driver-service/
Refactor notification message templates in notification-service/src/index.ts
Add new RabbitMQ event handlers in existing services
agent_forbidden_operations: Modify or delete .env.example or .env files containing secrets
Alter the openapi.yaml specification without explicit instruction
Change database migration files or DDL scripts
Modify JWT secret configuration or authentication bypass logic
Remove or weaken the OrderStatus state transition validation
Disable or remove Stripe payment integration
Change RabbitMQ exchange names or routing key conventions without coordinating across all services
potential_pitfalls: Each service uses a different language and build tool — changes must use the correct toolchain (Maven for order-service, Gradle for api-gateway, pip for inventory-service, go for driver-service, npm for notification-service).
The API Gateway's build.gradle.kts uses Kotlin DSL, not Groovy — syntax differs significantly.
Order Service uses Java 21 features (switch expressions, sealed patterns) — code must target Java 21+.
Inventory Service uses async SQLAlchemy (asyncpg) and async Redis — all DB/cache operations must be awaited.
The Driver Service uses raw net/http (no framework) with manual routing in ServeHTTP — no router library is available.
Stock reservation in the Inventory Service requires acquiring a Redis distributed lock before decrementing; skipping this causes race conditions.
OrderStatus.isValidTransition enforces a strict state machine — invalid transitions throw IllegalStateException at the service layer.
The Notification Service idempotency guard is an in-memory Set that is lost on restart — do not rely on it for durable deduplication.
All inter-service communication in production goes through the API Gateway which injects X-Customer-Id after JWT validation — direct service-to-service calls bypass auth.
The order-service uses Stripe manual capture (CaptureMethod.MANUAL) — payment intents must be explicitly captured after order acceptance.
Environment variables differ between docker-compose context (e.g., SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL) and .env.example (e.g., POSTGRES_HOST) — check both when adding config.
